hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: Checking In and Speaking Up -- Asking for Clarification in Global Meetings
lesson.menu_desc: O remédio para o travamento na call: as três caixas de ferramentas -- pedir para esclarecer, segurar a palavra e ganhar tempo -- para nunca mais ficar em silêncio numa reunião com a Global -- 37 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- Checking In and Speaking Up
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Lesson 14 -- Checking In and Speaking Up

listenings.text: Hi Emmanuele, David here from the Global team. Quick one before tomorrow's call. We are moving the review to eleven, and I am going to share a new deck tonight. Two things I need from you. First, a short update on the Brazil numbers, just the headline, not every figure. Second, your main concern for the second half, so we can put it on the agenda. If anything is not clear on the deck, please do not stay quiet on the call. Just ask me to clarify. It is a long agenda and we are going to move fast, so it is completely fine to say, sorry, can you repeat that. See you tomorrow.

Okay everyone, we are almost out of time, so let me wrap up. Let me summarize the three action points. First, Emmanuele is going to send the Brazil update by Thursday. Second, the access team is going to follow up on the public market. And third, we are going to add the pricing question to the agenda for next week. It is too big for today, so let's take that offline. One last thing. If I explained anything too fast, please write to me. I am happy to clarify. Thank you all. Good meeting.

extra_audio.text: Sorry, Marc, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat the last part, please? And what do you mean by the new figures? Just to clarify, you need them by Friday? Let me think for a second before I answer. I have one concern, and I am going to follow up this week.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to explain something again so it is clear
a worry or a problem you want to talk about
to finish a meeting or a topic
when two people understand the same thing in different ways
to stop someone while they are speaking
to bring up a point or a question in a meeting
comments about your work, to help you improve
to say the main points again, in a short way

reading.rtitle: The Bravest Sentence in a Meeting
paras: In a global meeting, many people stay quiet. Not because they have nothing to say, but because they are afraid of one small thing: asking a question. They think, "If I ask them to repeat, they will see that I did not understand." So they nod, they smile, and they lose the information. And later, after the call, they finally understand the point, alone at their desk. Too late.

But the truth is the opposite. The strongest person in the room is the one who says, "Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?" Asking for clarification is not a sign of weak English. It is a sign of a professional who wants to get it right. Every good meeting is full of these sentences: "What do you mean by that?", "Just to clarify...", "If I understand correctly, you need the report on Friday." They are not weakness. They are control.

There is a second skill, and it is just as brave: holding the floor. Sometimes you start to speak, and someone interrupts you. The easy thing is to stop and go quiet. The professional thing is to say, calmly, "Let me finish, please," or "Can I just add one thing?" And when you do not know the answer yet, you do not freeze -- you buy time: "That's a good question. Let me think for a second." None of this is about perfect grammar. It is about staying in the conversation, instead of disappearing from it.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: In a global meeting, it is safer to stay quiet and understand the points later, after the call.
Asking for clarification and holding the floor are signs of a strong professional, not of weak English -- they keep you in the conversation.
You should only speak in a meeting when your grammar is perfect.

items: The text says people stay quiet in meetings because they have nothing to say.
The opposite: "Not because they have nothing to say, but because they are afraid of asking a question."
According to the text, asking someone to repeat is a sign of weak English.
The text says the opposite: "Asking for clarification is not a sign of weak English. It is a sign of a professional who wants to get it right."
The text says that when someone interrupts you, the professional thing is to stop and go quiet.
It says the professional thing is to say, calmly, "Let me finish, please," or "Can I just add one thing?"
According to the text, when you do not know the answer yet, you can buy time instead of freezing.
"you do not freeze -- you buy time: 'That's a good question. Let me think for a second.'"

that." If a word is not clear, you ask: "What do you
by that?" To check the plan, you begin with: "Just to
..." If someone interrupts you, you say: "Let me
, please." And if you don't know the answer yet, you

gapfill.text: Three small toolkits save every meeting. To CLARIFY, you have: "Could you repeat that?", "What do you mean by...?", "Just to clarify...". To HOLD THE FLOOR, you have: "Let me finish, please", "What I mean is...", "Can I add one thing?". To BUY TIME, you have: "That's a good question. Let me think for a second." Learn one phrase from each box by heart, and you never freeze on the call again.

lf.title: Which tool is it -- clarify, hold the floor, or buy time?
items: What do you mean by 'the gap'?
Let me think for a second.

lf.text: A and C ASK for clarification -- they bring the information back to you. B holds the floor -- it keeps your turn when someone jumps in. D buys time -- it gives your brain two seconds instead of a panic. Different words, one single goal every time: stay in the conversation, do not disappear from it. This is not grammar. This is control.

bank.label: Useful language for a global meeting
items: Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?
What do you mean by that, exactly?
Just to clarify, you need the report by Friday, correct?
If I understand correctly, we are launching in March.
Let me finish, please -- I have one more point.
That's a good question. Let me think for a second.
Can we take that offline and follow up after the meeting?

items.situation: The Global colleague speaks very fast and you miss a number. What do you say?
tips: Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?
Could you repeat the last number, please?

items.situation: He uses a word you don't know: "churn". Ask him to explain it.
tips: What do you mean by "churn", exactly?

items.situation: You want to check you understood the deadline. Confirm it in one sentence.
tips: Just to clarify, you need the report by Friday, correct?
If I understand correctly, the deadline is Friday.

items.situation: You are speaking and someone interrupts you. Hold the floor.
tips: Let me finish, please -- I have one more point.
Can I just finish this point, please?

items.situation: Marc asks a question and you don't know the answer yet. Buy time.
tips: That's a good question. Let me think for a second.
Let me check and get back to you.

items.situation: The topic is too big for now. Move it to a later conversation.
tips: Can we take that offline and follow up later?
Let's take that offline and put it on the agenda for next week.
